unassigned.label: Talkie
unassigned.detail:
[ask:f-mqckzdvr-jqfe] TLK-030 review — runtime/perf/regression focus. Reviewed the actual files, not just the spec.

Runtime/model: Claude Code on Opus 4.8 (claude-opus-4-8). "high" reasoning effort was requested; I can't introspect the effort knob from inside the runtime, so treat that as assumed-as-instructed, not verified.

== FINDINGS (most severe first, with refs) ==

1) #1 runtime trap: web-overlay assets resolve via Bundle.main, not Bundle.module.
Both markup web controllers locate overlay.{html,css,js} relative to the host app bundle:
- CaptureMarkupWebSession.swift:286-287
- LiveCaptureMarkupOverlayController.swift:159-160 (bundledMarkupDirectory)
The spec's Phase 4 names BOTH as TalkieCaptureKit candidates (spec L135, L137). Moving either down without also moving the assets + switching to Bundle.module compiles clean and fails ONLY at runtime: bundledMarkupDirectory() -> nil -> logs "resources missing" -> overlay silently no-ops. Invisible to compiler + typecheck.
Worse for Agent: spec wires TalkieAgent -> TalkieCaptureKit, and TalkieAgent ALSO resolves via Bundle.main (TalkieAgentRuntime.swift:281, AgentRuntimeClient.swift:361) with its own markup entry (AgentVoiceTool.runCaptureMarkupOpen). Bundle.main there = the Agent bundle, which doesn't ship these assets. Bundle.module fixes BOTH and is strictly more correct. Pattern is already proven in this exact package (TalkieContextRoots.swift:25, TalkieKitFonts.swift:33). Mitigation: any controller move must, in the SAME commit, add overlay/* as a .copy resource in the new target AND swap Bundle.main -> Bundle.module. This is the spec's "resource assumptions" risk but more severe/concrete than stated.

2) The markup MODEL + RENDERER is genuinely zero-runtime-risk — and is already in the package.
CaptureMarkupDocument/Layer/Rect/Point + Renderer + Storage are pure value types, Codable over JSON with explicit string CodingKeys, persisted as .markup.json sidecars via JSONEncoder/Decoder (Storage L25-41), bridged to JS via JSONSerialization (LiveOverlay L221-228). Module name is NOT part of any serialized form, and there is NO NSKeyedArchiver/NSClassFromString/NSCoding anywhere in Services or TalkieKit (grep empty). So moving these types between modules cannot break on-disk sidecars or the JS bridge. Renderer is CoreGraphics/CoreText only. CaptureMarkupTests already covers JSON round-trip + legacy tolerance + version migration. -> This is the safe first slice, and safer than the spec's Phase-3 ranking implies.

3) The capture CONTROLLERS are NOT a safe first slice — they're singleton-saturated.
App-service coupling found in the candidate set:
- SettingsManager.shared (CaptureHUDPanel:129, CaptureHUDController:44)
- FeatureFlags.shared (CaptureBarController:37, CaptureHUDController:25, app ScreenCaptureOverlay:281)
- SelectionTray.shared (CaptureBarController:39, CaptureHUDController:27)
- ScreenshotCaptureService.shared (WallpaperLuminanceSampler:23)
- LLM/transcription stack in CaptureMarkupPanelChrome (LLMProviderRegistry/LLMConfig/EphemeralTranscriber .shared, :896-1010)
The spec only flags ScreenRecordingController.shared (L141); real coupling is broader. Each downward move needs a dependency inversion (protocol/closure), and each inversion is a behavior-change surface = regression risk. By contrast LiveCaptureMarkupOverlayController has ZERO .shared hits — already closure-based (onLayersChanged/onDone/onCancel) — so structurally ready EXCEPT for finding #1.

4) Keep static; treat Rule 5 ("dynamic only after measuring") as a hard gate, not a footnote.
Package.swift declares an automatic .library (no type:), static-linked into the app. Adding sibling targets (Core/Data/Design/Capture) in the same package costs ~0 at launch (code stays in the main image, dead-strippable, no extra dyld loads). The ONLY runtime-perf regression lever in this whole plan is flipping to dynamic frameworks: each adds a dyld image load + embed/sign at launch = measurable cold-launch cost on macOS. Rule 5 is load-bearing — gate it on a launch-time measurement.

5) Keep the render loop inside one module.
Cross-module non-@inlinable public calls lose cross-module inlining/specialization (micro-cost, only matters in hot loops). The renderer's per-layer loop is the one hot-ish path, but it's export/markup-time, not per-frame, so it's fine either way — just don't split the layer types into DataKit and the renderer into CaptureKit; that puts a module boundary inside the loop for no benefit. Move CaptureMarkupDocument/Layer + Renderer together.

== SAFEST FIRST EXTRACTION (ordered) ==
1. Split a TalkieCaptureKit target inside the EXISTING TalkieKit/Package.swift and move the model+renderer+storage trio into it (they're already in the package — this is a target split, not a move out of the app). Zero runtime coupling. Validate via existing CaptureMarkupTests under xcodebuild (swift test skips #if canImport(AppKit)/#if DEBUG — must be xcodebuild on macOS, per your TalkieKit-verify rule).
2. Then LiveCaptureMarkupOverlayController, PAIRED with the resource fix (#1): move overlay.{html,css,js} into the target as .copy + Bundle.module, same commit, then runtime-smoke the live-recording markup path.
3. Defer CaptureMarkupWebSession (still-image editor) — it drags in CaptureMarkupPanelChrome's LLM/transcription singletons.

== STAYS APP-SIDE (first pass) ==
CaptureBarController, CaptureBarPanel, CaptureHUDController, CaptureHUDPanel, WallpaperLuminanceSampler, CaptureMarkupPanelChrome, and anything touching ScreenRecordingController.shared. Invert these only when there's a concrete reason.

== MEASUREMENTS ==
Spec's Phase-1 build metrics are good; the gap is regression detection (failures here are runtime-only, invisible to build). Add per-extraction:
- Runtime smoke gate each move: trigger screenshot + live-recording markup -> assert overlay.html actually loaded (promote the Log(.ui).error("resources missing") path to a hard failure in a smoke check).
- Renderer golden-image test: draw one of each layer kind, export PNG, diff vs golden. CaptureMarkupTests covers JSON but NOT pixels, so a renderer regression after the move is currently uncaught. Also exercises font resolution (package bundles Resources/Fonts).
- Legacy sidecar decode: load a pre-move .markup.json, confirm decode — partly covered already by the legacy-tolerance tests.
- Baseline app cold-launch now, so the static->dynamic call later is data-driven.

Next owner: implementer. No handoff blocker. The one non-obvious must-do: bundle #1's resource fix into the SAME commit as any controller move; everything else is mechanical. Can go deeper on any single extraction if useful.

unassigned.detail:
[ask:f-mqmst6x0-s0xl] Brainstormed + grounded in the real code. Full brief w/ code anchors + 5 sketches: docs/hyperspace-grid-snappiness.md

Governing idea: felt latency binds to the EARLIEST feedback, not when pixels finish. The AX move is 40-120ms you can't erase, so acknowledge on key-down and let windows catch up.

QUICK WINS (ship this week, ~low risk):
- Q1 Haptic on key-down — zero haptics in the codebase today. NSHapticFeedbackManager .alignment fired at the G keysite (WindowMotionMode.swift:833) BEFORE distributeGroup() = exact Loop/Magnet snap feel, ~10 lines, biggest bang.
- Q2 Fix the tap sound: today playTap (DiagnosticLog.swift:162) does async + stop()+play() + cold first-play. Pre-warm on mode entry, fire synchronously on key-down, drop the async hop. Sound+haptic land on the press, not the landing.
- Q3 Shrink the SLS freeze: batchMoveAndRaiseWindows does the slow kAXWindows enumeration INSIDE the freeze (WindowTiler.swift:1776). Resolve AX before freezing; relayoutGroup already has the el from recordOriginal (:1113) so you can skip the 2nd AX pass entirely.
- Q4 Stop activating every app: it calls app.activate() per pid (:1818) = focus churn + Space flicker + overlay can lose key. Raise via AX (already done :1806), activate ONLY the intended-frontmost app once.
- Q5 Overlay snap-pop: move is a teleport, so add the SENSE of motion in the cheap chrome layer — scale overshoot 1.0->1.045->1.0 + green edge flash converging on each slot (~130ms, Core Animation, off the AX path).

BIGGER BETS:
- B1 Ghost-slot anticipation (headliner): on G, instantly paint target slots and animate them filling over ~140ms while the real batch runs underneath. Decouples felt-speed from AX latency entirely. Build on Q5's overlay.
- B2 Precompute grid frames + AX element cache on selection change, so commit = pure frozen writes.
- B3 Staggered cascade fill of the overlay tiles (~12ms/cell, reading order) for the deliberate-fast Arc/Raycast feel — real windows still move simultaneously.

AVOID (these ADD latency/jank): animating many REAL windows via per-tick AX writes (RealWindowAnimator's 60fps Timer is fine for 1 window, stutters across N — keep the teleport, animate the overlay); AX enum / DesktopModel.poll() on the commit path (already deferred — keep it off); async/stop+play sound; per-app activate; Timer-driven overlay anim (use CADisplayLink/Core Animation); dwell — WindowHighlight defaults to 0.9s dwell+0.3s fade, wrong for a snap (want ~120-180ms total).

Ship order: Q1+Q2 (one keysite change) -> Q3+Q4 (real latency cut) -> Q5 -> B1.

Next owner = you (Grok) for impl; doc has copy-pasteable Swift sketches for all of the above. Happy to draft the actual diffs for Q1-Q4 if you want — just say the word.

sessions.label: Hudson
sessions.detail:
Verdict: **simplify / defer three-id segmentation.** Do not introduce `scoutSessionId` + `refSessionId` + `harnessSessionId` as first-class v0 fields yet. Reclaim `harnessSessionId` for provider truth, move tmux/local attach data under `terminalSurface`, and add only the minimum Scout-owned row id if there is no existing stable `agent.id`.

Top 3 risks:

1. **`scoutSessionId` looks redundant with `agent.id` / endpoint id.**  
   The proposal says “Scout-owned durable session identity” assigned at endpoint/card registration. That is dangerously close to the existing Agent/Card/endpoint identity. If it is not a concrete lifecycle different from `agent.id`, it becomes another almost-id every caller must learn. For local v0, one Scout-side identity is enough. If `agent.id` is not durable enough, fix that invariant instead of adding a second id.

2. **`refSessionId` is a sanctioned grab bag.**  
   “tmux, remote node, runtime handle, TBD” is not an invariant; it is an escape hatch. Adding `refKind` helps diagnostics but does not stop consumers from binding lanes/tail/attach to the wrong thing again. If the only known local v0 reference is tmux/zellij, name it where it belongs: `terminalSurface.sessionName` / `terminalSurface.kind`. Do not mint a generic `refSessionId` until there are at least two real non-terminal reference types with common behavior.

3. **Migration cost exceeds current bug shape.**  
   The actual failures were: `harnessSessionId` polluted with tmux refs, and `externalSessionId` ambiguously naming provider truth. Those are solved by a narrower rename/cleanup:
   - `externalSessionId` → `harnessSessionId`
   - remove tmux refs from `harnessSessionId`
   - lane/tail bind only on `harnessSessionId`
   - terminal relay vocabulary stays in terminal relay structures  
   Three ids touch protocol, sqlite, web projection, runtime, lanes, macOS, and MCP. That is too much blast radius for a local pilot.

Smallest honest model for local v0:

```ts
Agent {
  id: string;                 // Scout-owned durable agent/session/card identity
  harness?: HarnessKind;       // claude/codex/etc
  harnessSessionId?: string;   // provider conversation/session id only
  harnessLogPath?: string;     // if needed for observe/tail
  terminalSurface?: {
    kind: "tmux" | "zellij";
    sessionName: string;       // former relay-* / endpoint tmux name
    paneId?: string;
    nodeId?: string;
  };
}
```

Counter-proposal field list:

- Keep/strengthen: `agent.id` as the Scout durable identity.
- Rename: `externalSessionId` → `harnessSessionId`.
- Move: tmux/relay refs → `terminalSurface.sessionName`.
- Optional later: `runtimeRef` only when there is a proven non-terminal attach abstraction.
- Do **not** add `scoutSessionId` unless you can prove `agent.id` cannot serve.
- Do **not** add generic `refSessionId` for v0.

unassigned.detail:
Fixed and verified end-to-end — it was a **stale install**, not a code or contract bug.

---

**HudsonKit flags export mismatch — resolved ✅**

**Intended import contract: root is correct — keep importing from `@hudsonkit`.** hudsonkit `0.2.1` deliberately re-exports the *entire* flags runtime + types from the bare root (`createFlagRegistry`, `createFlagResolver`, `parseFeatureFlagUrl`, `useFlag`/`useFeatureFlags`/…, and the storage helpers incl. `readFeatureFlagLocalState`) — that root re-export was an explicit consumer-feedback fix in this release. **Do not switch `scout-flags.ts` to the `/flags` subpath** (it exists too, but root is what your code uses and it's fully supported). Both `scout-flags.ts` and hudsonkit's exports were already correct.

**Root cause:** OpenScout's `node_modules` had a **stale pre-flags `hudsonkit@0.2.0`**; the published `0.2.1` (which carries the root flag surface) hadn't been extracted. So vite's `@hudsonkit` → `require.resolve('hudsonkit')` landed on the old flags-less `dist/index.js`. (Note: `0.2.1` *is* now published to npm — it went out after the SCO-065 ship turn — and I packed the published tarball to confirm it has all six flag fns + the `./flags` subpath.)

**Fix applied (OpenScout side):** ran `bun install` in `/Users/art/dev/openscout` — your `bun.lock` already pinned `hudsonkit@^0.2.1`, so this just materialized `0.2.1` into `node_modules/.bun/`. Verified:
- `require.resolve('hudsonkit')` from `packages/web` → `…/.bun/hudsonkit@0.2.1/…/dist/index.js`, and `createFlagRegistry`/`createFlagResolver`/`parseFeatureFlagUrl`/`readFeatureFlagLocalState` all resolve as functions.
- `bun run --cwd packages/web build:client` → **builds clean: 3581 modules, 4.70s** (no "not exported" error; `RepoDiffViewer` chunk and all). 

No source changes on either repo, no lockfile content change — just the install refresh. You can also now **drop `OPENSCOUT_WEB_HUDSONKIT_SOURCE=auto` entirely**; it's building against published `0.2.1`. You're unblocked.
